corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Table 1 compares test accuracies of GINs (Sum-MLP), other GNN variants, as well as the state-of-the-art baselines.
First, GINs, especially GIN-0, outperform (or achieve comparable performance as) the less powerful GNN variants on all the 9 datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
First, GINs, especially GIN-0, outperform (or achieve comparable performance as) the less powerful GNN variants on all the 9 datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
Here, GINs and sum-aggregation GNNs accurately capture the graph structure and significantly outperform other models.
Comparing GINs (GIN-0 and GIN-), we observe that GIN-0 slightly but consistently outperforms GIN-.
An interesting direction for future work is to go beyond neighborhood aggregation (or message passing) in order to pursue possibly even more powerful architectures for learning with graphs.
We start with an overview of our framework for analyzing the expressive power of GNNs.
Under the GIN framework, we consider two variants: (1) a GIN that learns in Eq.
Here mean-1-layer and max-1-layer correspond to GCN and GraphSAGE, respectively, up to minor architecture modifications.
Dataset statistics are summarized in Table , and more details of the data can be found in Appendix I.
For example, on IMDBBINARY, none of the models can perfectly fit the training set, and the GNNs achieve at most the same training accuracy as the WL kernel.

Even if node degrees are provided as input features, mean-based GNNs perform much worse than sum-based GNNs (the accuracy of the GNN with mean-MLP aggregation is 71.2±4.6% on REDDIT-BINARY and 41.3±2.1% on REDDIT-MULTI5K).
COLLAB is a scientific collaboration dataset, derived from 3 public collaboration datasets, namely, High Energy Physics, Condensed Matter Physics and Astro Physics.
MUTAG is a dataset of 188 mutagenic aromatic and heteroaromatic nitro compounds with 7 discrete labels.
PROTEINS is a dataset where nodes are secondary structure elements (SSEs) and there is an edge between two nodes if they are neighbors in the amino-acid sequence or in 3D space.
PTC is a dataset of 344 chemical compounds that reports the carcinogenicity for male and female rats and it has 19 discrete labels.

NCI1 is a dataset made publicly available by the National Cancer Institute (NCI) and is a subset of balanced datasets of chemical compounds screened for ability to suppress or inhibit the growth of a panel of human tumor cell lines, having 37 discrete labels.
Models with higher representational power should have higher training set accuracy.
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are an effective framework for representation learning of graphs.
Many GNN variants have been proposed and have achieved state-of-the-art results on both node and graph classification tasks.
Here, we present a theoretical framework for analyzing the expressive power of GNNs to capture different graph structures.
Here, we present a theoretical framework for analyzing the expressive power of GNNs to capture different graph structures.

Our results characterize the discriminative power of popular GNN variants, such as Graph Convolutional Networks and GraphSAGE, and show that they cannot learn to distinguish certain simple graph structures.
We then develop a simple architecture that is provably the most expressive among the class of GNNs and is as powerful as the Weisfeiler-Lehman graph isomorphism test.
